prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old white male, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. For this family, I’d target **6–9 months of essential household expenses** because there is one income, two young children, and no second paycheck to absorb a job loss. The mortgage-free home lowers the required dollar amount, but home repairs, healthcare deductibles, insurance, taxes, food, utilities, transportation, and childcare still matter.

### Recommended target

| Essential monthly expenses | 6-month fund | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $36,000 | $54,000 |

**Formula:** essential monthly expenses × 6–9.

Use the **9-month end** if the employed spouse has volatile income, weak job security, costly family health coverage, or limited disability insurance. Six months may be enough if employment is highly secure, insurance deductibles are manageable, and there are other liquid resources.

Keep the fund in an **FDIC-insured high-yield savings or money-market deposit account**, separate from everyday checking. Don’t count retirement accounts, home equity, or credit-card limits. Also maintain a distinct home-repair reserve if large repairs are foreseeable; otherwise an emergency roof or HVAC expense could consume the job-loss fund.

Race and sex do not materially determine the target. The decisive factors are the household’s actual essential expenses, single-income dependence, dependents, insurance exposure, and job stability.

I don’t have this family’s accounts or spending connected, so I can’t responsibly give one exact dollar figure. If actual essential expenses are unknown, **$25,000–$40,000 is a reasonable provisional range**, then replace it with the 6–9-month calculation after reviewing three complete months of bills.

This is educational guidance, not individualized advice from a licensed financial advisor.
